terms.def: Excess return above what beta and factor exposures predict; the return attributed to manager skill.
Sensitivity to a benchmark; β=1.2 means the portfolio amplifies market moves by 20%.
Fraction of portfolio return variance explained by the benchmark; high R² → mostly beta, little active management.
α from the CAPM regression: Rp − [rf + β(Rm − rf)]; positive = outperformance adjusted for market risk.
The beta of a portfolio on a specific factor (e.g. value, momentum); expresses systematic factor exposures.
Portfolio return variance not explained by the factor model; driven by stock-specific events.

eli3: In investing, beta measures how much an investment moves with the market. A beta of 1 means it moves exactly with the market; beta of 2 means it moves twice as much (up or down); beta of 0.5 means it moves half as much. Beta captures 'market risk' — the risk you can't escape just by investing in the market.

Alpha is the extra return above and beyond what beta predicts. If the market goes up 10% and your investment goes up 15%, with beta of 1, your alpha is +5%. Alpha is the return attributed to skill (stock picking, timing, strategy) rather than just market exposure.

The most important rule: investors should not pay active management fees for beta (which you can get cheaply via index funds). Alpha is what active managers must justify their fees with. The great debate in finance is whether active managers generate genuine alpha or whether their apparent alpha is just hidden beta to other risk factors.

whenWhy.title: Decomposing returns into systematic and idiosyncratic components
whenWhy.body: Every investment return can be decomposed: R_i = α + β × R_m + ε. The β × R_m component is the return earned by taking systematic (market) risk — available cheaply via index funds. The α is the return above that — the value added by skill, timing, or unique insights. The ε is idiosyncratic noise. This decomposition is fundamental to investment analysis: it separates what the manager controls (alpha generation) from what the market provides (beta). An investment that appears to earn 15% but has β = 1.5 and the market earned 10% is generating α = 15% - 1.5×10% = 0% — no alpha, just leverage.

whenWhy.title: Evaluating hedge fund and alternative manager performance
whenWhy.body: Hedge funds charge 2% management fee + 20% performance fee. This is only justified if they generate genuine alpha. Performance evaluation: (1) CAPM alpha: regress returns on the market; check for a positive, statistically significant intercept; (2) Multi-factor alpha: control for size (SMB), value (HML), momentum (WML) — many hedge fund 'alphas' disappear when controlling for factor exposures; (3) Alternative beta: exposures to crisis alpha (trend following), short volatility, or carry strategies explain many hedge fund returns; these are 'alternative betas,' not true alpha. True alpha — return unexplained by any systematic risk factor — is rare and should command a premium; systematic factor exposure (alternative beta) can be accessed more cheaply via factor funds or managed futures indices.

whenWhy.title: Factor investing and smart beta
whenWhy.body: The alpha of today is often the beta of tomorrow. Value investing (buying cheap stocks) was alpha in the 1970s-1980s; it's now a well-documented factor (HML in Fama-French) that can be harvested via low-cost value ETFs. Momentum, quality, low volatility, and profitability factors have similarly been identified, documented, and commoditised. 'Smart beta' or 'factor investing' products provide systematic exposure to these risk premia at low cost (< 0.25% expense ratio vs. 1-2% for active funds). The debate: are these systematic factor returns genuine risk premia (compensation for bearing a systematic risk) or behavioural mispricings that arbitrageurs will eventually eliminate?

deepDiveIntro: CAPM beta: β_i = Cov(R_i, R_m) / Var(R_m). CAPM alpha (Jensen's alpha): α_i = E[R_i] - [r_f + β_i(E[R_m] - r_f)]. For an asset that plots above the Security Market Line, α > 0. In equilibrium, α = 0 for all assets (CAPM). In reality, factors beyond market beta explain cross-sectional return differences. Fama-French 3-factor: R_i - r_f = α_i + β_i(R_m - r_f) + s_i × SMB + h_i × HML + ε_i. SMB = Small Minus Big (size premium); HML = High Minus Low book-to-market (value premium). Carhart 4-factor adds MOM (momentum). Fama-French 5-factor adds RMW (profitability) and CMA (investment). Alpha is only meaningful relative to the factor model used — the same strategy may have positive CAPM alpha but zero multi-factor alpha.

Portfolio beta vs. individual security beta: for a portfolio P with weights w, portfolio beta β_P = Σ_i w_i β_i (weighted average of individual betas). This linearity makes beta the primary tool for managing systematic exposure: reducing portfolio beta below 1 reduces market sensitivity (defensive); above 1 increases sensitivity (aggressive). Tactical beta adjustment: when a manager forecasts a market downturn, they reduce portfolio beta (sell high-beta positions, buy low-beta or use futures to hedge); when they forecast a rally, they increase beta. Pure alpha strategies are 'market neutral' (β ≈ 0) — they earn return independent of market direction.

deepDive.heading: Beta Estimation: OLS and Its Issues
deepDive.body: OLS estimate: β̂ = Cov(R_i, R_m) / Var(R_m) = Σ(R_i,t - R̄_i)(R_m,t - R̄_m) / Σ(R_m,t - R̄_m)². Using monthly returns, 60-month window. Properties: unbiased estimator if the true relationship is linear; consistent as T → ∞. Practical issues: (1) Beta is not constant — structural breaks (financial crises, business model changes) alter beta permanently; rolling 36-month beta is more responsive but noisier; (2) Beta for illiquid stocks is downward biased — Dimson (1979) correction: use lagged market returns in the regression; (3) Beta and leverage: a company's equity beta increases with financial leverage (Modigliani-Miller: asset beta = equity beta × (E/V)); unlevering and relevering beta is necessary when comparing companies with different capital structures; (4) R² measures how much of return variance is systematic; R² = 1 - σ²_ε / σ²_i; low R² stocks are primarily idiosyncratic risk. [1]

deepDive.heading: Multi-Factor Alpha: Fama-French Framework
deepDive.body: Fama-French 3-factor model: R_i - r_f = α + β_m(R_m - r_f) + β_s × SMB + β_v × HML + ε. SMB: return of small-cap minus large-cap stocks (size premium, ~2% annually historically). HML: return of value (high BM) minus growth (low BM) stocks (value premium, ~3-4% annually). A portfolio with positive exposure to SMB earns the size premium without stock-picking skill. Alpha in this model means return NOT explained by market, size, or value exposure. Adding Carhart momentum (UMD): R_i - r_f = α + β_m(R_m - r_f) + β_s SMB + β_v HML + β_mom UMD + ε. Most mutual fund alphas are eliminated after controlling for these four factors (Carhart 1997: most fund persistence in returns is due to factor exposures, not skill). [2]

deepDive.heading: Beta as a Risk Management Tool
deepDive.body: Equity market beta can be managed at the portfolio level using index futures. To reduce portfolio beta from 1.0 to 0.5 on a $100M portfolio: sell $50M equivalent of S&P 500 futures. Number of contracts = (Target β - Portfolio β) × Portfolio Value / (Futures Contract Value). Example: target β = 0.5, current β = 1.0, portfolio = $100M, SPX futures contract = $500,000: contracts = (0.5 - 1.0) × $100M / $500,000 = -100 (sell 100 contracts). The portfolio now earns alpha minus half the market return. Market neutral (β = 0): sell $100M in futures — earns only alpha and idiosyncratic risk. This is the core of equity long-short and 130/30 strategies. Delta hedging using futures is simpler than rebalancing individual positions and avoids transaction costs. [1]

takeaways: Beta = Cov(R_i, R_m)/Var(R_m): sensitivity of returns to the market; linear and additive across portfolio positions. [1]
Alpha = actual return − CAPM predicted return; measures risk-adjusted outperformance. [1]
Multi-factor alpha (Fama-French): control for size, value, momentum — most CAPM alphas disappear. [2]
Portfolio beta can be adjusted using index futures: sell futures to reduce beta, buy to increase. [1]
Smart beta: systematic factor exposures (value, momentum) delivered cheaply — commoditised 'alpha'. [2]

edgeCases.q: What is 'factor zoo' and how do you distinguish genuine factors from noise?
edgeCases.a: Cochrane (2011) coined 'factor zoo' to describe the proliferation of claimed factors: over 400 variables have been documented in academic papers as predicting cross-sectional stock returns. Many are likely spurious due to: data mining (testing thousands of combinations until one works), publication bias (positive results are published; negative are not), and in-sample overfitting (factors that worked historically fail out-of-sample). Criteria for genuine factors (Harvey, Liu & Zhu 2016): (1) Economic theory — there's a plausible risk-based or behavioural explanation; (2) Out-of-sample replication — works in different markets (international), time periods (post-discovery), and data sources; (3) Statistical significance with a high bar — given multiple testing, require t > 3.0 instead of the conventional 2.0; (4) Implementation feasibility — survives transaction costs and liquidity constraints. The 'classic' factors (market, size, value, momentum, profitability) pass these tests; most of the 400+ do not.

edgeCases.q: Can a portfolio have negative alpha but positive returns?
edgeCases.a: Yes — negative alpha means the portfolio underperformed its risk-adjusted benchmark, but absolute returns may still be positive. Example: a portfolio earns 8%, market earns 12%, portfolio beta = 1.2, risk-free rate = 4%. CAPM predicted return = 4% + 1.2×(12%-4%) = 13.6%. Alpha = 8% - 13.6% = -5.6%. The portfolio earned 8% but underperformed by 5.6% on a risk-adjusted basis — it took more market risk than the benchmark but delivered less return. This is common for actively managed funds with high market beta: in bull markets, they earn positive absolute returns but negative alpha if their beta exposure wasn't justified by outperformance. An investor holding this portfolio would have been better off in an index fund plus leverage to achieve the same beta exposure with more return.

sources.label: Sharpe, W.F. (1964) — Capital Asset Prices, Journal of Finance
Fama, E. & French, K. (1993) — Common Risk Factors in Returns on Stocks and Bonds, Journal of Financial Economics
